tex:
A shop sells food and drink.

(a) Bananas cost \$1.20 per kilogram and apples cost \$2.25 per bag.

Work out the total cost of 3.5 kg of bananas and 2 bags of apples.

(b) Students receive a 10\% discount on their shopping.
Before the discount, the cost of a student's shopping is \$16.80.

Work out the amount of the discount.

(c) The cost of a cabbage increases by 15\%.

Calculate the new price if the original price is \$1.80.

(d) Some customers have their shopping delivered to their home.
The cost is \$5 plus \$1.50 for each kilometre travelled from the shop to their home.

(i) (e) Show that the cost for a customer living 10 km from the shop is \$20.

(f) \begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

On the grid, draw a line to show the cost of having shopping delivered.

(g) A bottle of water costs \$1.55.
Suki has \$20.

Work out the maximum number of bottles Suki can buy and the change she receives.

(h) A farmer delivers eggs to the shop in trays of 50.
The eggs are then put into boxes of 12.
There are no eggs left in the trays and all of the egg boxes are full.

Work out the smallest possible number of eggs that the farmer delivers.

(i) The shop sells bottles of orange juice in three different sizes.

\begin{tabular}{ccc}
Bottle A & Bottle B & Bottle C \\
0.5 litres & 1.2 litres & 2 litres \\
\$1.30 & \$3.20 & \$5.25 \\
\end{tabular}
Work out which bottle is the best value.
Show how you decide.
texHtml:
A shop sells food and drink. (a) Bananas cost \$1.20 per kilogram and apples cost \$2.25 per bag. Work out the total cost of 3.5 kg of bananas and 2 bags of apples. (b) Students receive a 10\% discount on their shopping. Before the discount, the cost of a student's shopping is \$16.80. Work out the amount of the discount. (c) The cost of a cabbage increases by 15\%. Calculate the new price if the original price is \$1.80. (d) Some customers have their shopping delivered to their home. The cost is \$5 plus \$1.50 for each kilometre travelled from the shop to their home. (i) (e) Show that the cost for a customer living 10 km from the shop is \$20. (f) On the grid, draw a line to show the cost of having shopping delivered. (g) A bottle of water costs \$1.55. Suki has \$20. Work out the maximum number of bottles Suki can buy and the change she receives. (h) A farmer delivers eggs to the shop in trays of 50. The eggs are then put into boxes of 12. There are no eggs left in the trays and all of the egg boxes are full. Work out the smallest possible number of eggs that the farmer delivers. (i) The shop sells bottles of orange juice in three different sizes.
Bottle ABottle BBottle C
0.5 litres1.2 litres2 litres
\$1.30\$3.20\$5.25
Work out which bottle is the best value. Show how you decide.

tex:
The grid shows the first three diagrams in a sequence.
Each diagram is made using sticks.

\begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

(a) On the grid, draw Diagram 4.

(b) Complete the table.

\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
Diagram number & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
\hline
Number of sticks & 5 & 9 & 13 & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

(c) (i) (d) Find an expression, in terms of $n$, for the number of sticks in Diagram $n$.

(e) One of the diagrams has 73 sticks.

Work out its Diagram number.

(f) (i) (g) Show that the total number of sticks needed to make the first 3 diagrams is 27.

(h) The total number of sticks needed to make the first $k$ diagrams is $2k^2 + 3k$.

Show that this expression gives the correct total number of sticks needed to make the first 3 diagrams.

(i) Tobias wants to make the first 10 diagrams.
He has already made the first 3 diagrams.
He has 240 sticks left to make the remaining 7 diagrams.

Work out how many sticks he has left when all 10 diagrams are made.
texHtml:
The grid shows the first three diagrams in a sequence. Each diagram is made using sticks. (a) On the grid, draw Diagram 4. (b) Complete the table.
Diagram number12345
Number of sticks5913
(c) (i) (d) Find an expression, in terms of $n$, for the number of sticks in Diagram $n$. (e) One of the diagrams has 73 sticks. Work out its Diagram number. (f) (i) (g) Show that the total number of sticks needed to make the first 3 diagrams is 27. (h) The total number of sticks needed to make the first $k$ diagrams is $2k^2 + 3k$. Show that this expression gives the correct total number of sticks needed to make the first 3 diagrams. (i) Tobias wants to make the first 10 diagrams. He has already made the first 3 diagrams. He has 240 sticks left to make the remaining 7 diagrams. Work out how many sticks he has left when all 10 diagrams are made.

tex:
\begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

(a) Write down the equation of line $L$ in the form $y = mx + c$.

(b) (i) (c) Complete the table of values for $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$.

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
$x$ & $-2$ & $-1$ & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
\hline
$y$ & & 1 & $-3$ & $-5$ & $-5$ & & 1 & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

(d) On the grid, draw the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$ for $-2 \le x \le 5$.

(e) (i) (f) Write down the coordinates of the lowest point of the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$.

(g) On the grid, draw the line of symmetry of the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$.

(h) Write down the equation of the line of symmetry.

(i) Write down the coordinates of the point where line $L$ intersects the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$ for $x \ge 0$.

$y$1$-3$$-5$$-5$1
(d) On the grid, draw the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$ for $-2 \le x \le 5$. (e) (i) (f) Write down the coordinates of the lowest point of the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$. (g) On the grid, draw the line of symmetry of the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$. (h) Write down the equation of the line of symmetry. (i) Write down the coordinates of the point where line $L$ intersects the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$ for $x \ge 0$.

tex:
\begin{question*}{9}{a}The length, $l$ m, of a piece of wire is 18.7 metres, correct to the nearest 10 centimetres.

Complete the statement about the value of $l$.

(b)850 metres of wire has a mass of 130.5 kilograms.

Work out the length of wire, in metres, that has a mass of 900 grams.

(c)Aluminium is used to make the wire.
The mass of 1 cm$^3$ of aluminium is 2.7 grams.

Work out the mass, in grams, of 6000 cm$^3$ of aluminium.
Give your answer in standard form.

(d)A 12 metre length of wire increases in length to 12.017 metres when its temperature rises.

Calculate the percentage increase in the length of the wire.

\end{question*}
